It's quite rational to regulate frontier API models, especially to get more transparency for the government, without regulating open-source AI.

Here's why:

1. The most dangerous AI systems right now aren't open models. They're the large frontier LLM APIs distributed through coding tools and assistants, because:
- They're built in secret behind closed doors and stay total black boxes. Zero transparency on what they can or can't do, with "safeguards" that blur everyone's ability to even analyze them.
- They're built and controlled by a few profit-maximizing megacorps, concentrating unprecedented power in very few hands, with every incentive to downplay risks and overstate their safeguards.
- They're distributed to hundreds of millions, maybe billions, of people and trivially easy to run.

2. Open-weight models are orders of magnitude less risky:
- They're not as massively distributed or as easy to use, especially the big ones, as APIs and assistants.
- We (including governments) can quickly and accurately analyze what they're capable of, and for now everyone confirms they're not as good as the APIs at doing bad things.
- They're distributed to everyone, so defenders and law enforcement get as much access as attackers.

The cost-benefit analysis of regulation is completely different too. Regulating frontier APIs is relatively easy and low risk while regulating open source would be much more complex, less efficient, and orders of magnitude more costly.

Regulating frontier APIs would only potentially hurt a few megacorps, if it even hurts them, given all the marketing that it is already generating for them. They can afford armies of lawyers and absorb losing a few billion dollars, especially given they're on track to become some of the most valuable companies in history. 

Regulating open source, by contrast, would hurt the very people regulation is supposed to protect: small businesses, startups, researchers, nonprofits, universities, independent developers, and the broader public, while risking killing competition, slowing AI progress, and reducing transparency even more!

Today, we're excited to release DFD (Data Forcing Distillation), a single-line fix that restores diversity and fidelity in DMD for few-step video distillation, led by my PhD student @SiyiChen277112! It improves on text-to-video, image-to-video, and causal auto-regressive video generation. 

The idea is simple: directly introducing real video samples into the training objective during distillation, so that the student can be explicitly pulled toward the diverse, high-quality real data distribution. This is implemented as a single line of code change when computing teacher scores: instead of evaluating the teacher score at the student's own generation (original DMD), we evaluate it on a real sample and compute the discrepancy with the fake sample. This pulls the student toward modes it missed (mitigating mode collapse) and away from over-saturated modes absent in real data.

With this single-line change, we can purely discard the GAN loss, and restore the diversity/fidelity by finetuning the DMD checkpoint with only 100–300 steps. 

Everything is released. Code (including both inference & training), pretrained model, and data:
Paper: https://t.co/GyWdjZ1Gah
Project page: https://t.co/8xnKb3vTKY
Code, model, and data: https://t.co/WGyo0kPMb1

We also want to have a huge shout-out to the authors of FastGen (https://t.co/SnPsjXLPX4) and Self-Forcing (https://t.co/KN3nli41oG) for releasing their code, which makes our work possible!

#VideoGeneration #DiffusionModels #AI #OpenSource

Andrej Karpathy explains why RLHF needed a cheat code.

The naive version sounds simple: show humans model outputs and ask them to score which ones are good.

Then he does the math:

"I want to do 1,000 updates, where each update will be on 1,000 prompts, and for each prompt we're going to have 1,000 rollouts that we're scoring."

"I will need to ask a human to evaluate a joke a total of 1 billion times."

"That's a lot of people looking at really terrible jokes, so we don't want to do that."

The trick is indirection:

"We're going to involve humans just a little bit, and the way we cheat is that we basically train a whole separate neural network that we call a reward model."

That is the hidden abstraction behind RLHF:

Humans do not scale the feedback loop.
A model trained to imitate human preference scales it.

This everyone should KNOW IT.

GOOGLE BUILT 9 FREE AI TOOLS AND FORGOT TO TELL ANYONE.

Most people are paying for the exact same things.

↳ Google AI Studio: build your own AI
↳ NotebookLM: any doc becomes a research assistant
↳ Learn Your Way: AI that adapts to how you learn
↳ Nano Banana: text to images, free
↳ Google Opal: idea to working app, no code
↳ Google Pomelli: brand to social content, automatic
↳ Gemini Gems: your own personal AI assistant
↳ Google Whisk: remix any image with AI
↳ Google Lumiere: text to video, free

Midjourney: $10/month. Runway: $15/month. OpenAI: $20/month.

Google built all of this. Charged you nothing. Told nobody.

Bookmark this before your feed buries it.
